step.title: Step 23
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 52, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 51 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Wait, something strange happened. RIGHT moved the LEFT square from (9,24) to (9,29), not the right square! And UP didn't move anything.
So RIGHT moves the LEFT square, not the right square. This means:
- LEFT/RIGHT moves the left square (or the square that's more to the left)
- UP/DOWN moves... let me think about this
Actually, looking at the pattern:
- When both squares were at the bottom, UP moved the right square up
- When the left square was at (14,24) and right at (49,34), UP moved the left square up
- When the left square was at (9,24) and right at (44,34), RIGHT moved the left square right
So it seems like the directional keys move the square that CAN move in that direction, not a specific square. Or maybe they move the "active" square, and the active square changes based on some rule.
Wait, let me reconsider. Looking at the current state: left at (9,29), right at (44,34).
The left square moved from (9,24) to (9,29) with RIGHT. So RIGHT moves the left square right.
But earlier, when both squares were at the bottom, UP moved the right square up, not the left square. Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, I need to reconsider which square is "left" and which is "right". The squares are:
- Sky blue square 1: was at (49,19) initially, now at (9,29)
- Sky blue square 2: was at (49,39) initially, now at (44,34)
So square 1 (left initially) is now at (9,29), and square 2 (right initially) is at (44,34).
Looking at the movement history:
- Initial: (49,19) and (49,39)
- After some UPs: (44,19) and (49,39) - left square moved up
- After more UPs: (9,19) and (49,39) - left square at top
- After RIGHTs: (9,24) and (49,39) - left square moved right
- After DOWN: (14,24) and (49,34) - left square moved down, right square moved left??
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think I've been confusing which square is which. Let me look at the current state:
- Square at (9,29): this is in the left chamber (yellow background)
- Square at (44,34): this is in the right chamber (orange background)
The left chamber is on the yellow side (left half of the board), and the right chamber is on the orange side (right half).
So the square at (9,29) is in the LEFT chamber, and the square at (44,34) is in the RIGHT chamber.
Now, looking at the movements:
- RIGHT moved the left chamber square from (9,24) to (9,29)
- UP didn't move anything (both squares are blocked)
So it seems like:
- LEFT/RIGHT moves the square that can move horizontally
- UP/DOWN moves the square that can move vertically
Or maybe the keys control BOTH squares simultaneously, and each square moves if it can in that direction.
Let me test this hypothesis. If I press LEFT, the left chamber square should move from (9,29) back to (9,24).
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the earlier movements. When I pressed UP earlier, the left square moved from (14,24) to (9,24). And when I pressed UP again, nothing moved. This suggests that UP moves the square that can move up.
But earlier, when both squares were at the bottom, UP moved the right square from (49,39) to (44,39). So UP can move either square, depending on which one can move.
Actually, I think the rule is: each directional key moves ALL squares that can move in that direction. If multiple squares can move, they all move. If only one can move, only that one moves.
Let me verify: when I pressed RIGHT, the left square moved from (9,24) to (9,29). The right square at (44,34) didn't move, probably because it's blocked from moving right at that position.
OK so the rule is: each directional key moves all squares that can move in that direction.
Now, current state: left at (9,29), right at (44,34).
I need to figure out the goal. Let me think about what configurations might complete the level.
One possibility: both squares need to be at the top of their chambers. The left square is already at row 9 (top). The right square is at row 44. To get it to the top, I need to navigate it through the narrow middle section.
But earlier attempts to move the right square up failed because it was blocked. Let me try moving the right square RIGHT first (to col 39), then UP.
But wait, RIGHT moves the left square, not the right square. How do I move the right square independently?
Maybe I need to position the left square such that it's blocked from moving right, so that RIGHT moves the right square instead.
Or maybe the keys control a specific "active" square, and I need to switch which square is active.
Actually, let me re-examine. When I pressed RIGHT, the left square moved. But the right square was at (44,34). If the right square could also move right, it would have moved too. Let me check if the right square can move right from (44,34).
Looking at the ASCII, at row 44, the right chamber has black from col 34 to col 50. The square at (44,34) occupies cols 34-38. Moving right would put it at col 39, occupying cols 39-43. This should be possible since the chamber extends to col 50.
But the right square didn't move when I pressed RIGHT. This suggests that RIGHT only moves the left square, not both.
Hmm, maybe the rule is different. Let me think...
Actually, maybe the directional keys move the square that is "closest" to the direction. For example, RIGHT moves the rightmost square that can move right, or the leftmost square that can move right.
Or maybe there's only one "active" square at a time, and the active square is the one that was last moved.
Let me test: currently, the left square was last moved (by RIGHT). If I press RIGHT again, does the left square move again?

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left at (9,29), right at (44,34)
# Test: does RIGHT move the left square again?
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
positions = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        bid = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in bid]
        cols = [p[1] for p in bid]
        positions.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
print(f"After RIGHT: {sorted(positions)}")

# Try LEFT to move left square back
result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
positions = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        bid = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in bid]
        cols = [p[1] for p in bid]
        positions.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
print(f"After LEFT: {sorted(positions)}")

# Try DOWN to see if it moves left square
result = action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
positions = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        bid = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in bid]
        cols = [p[1] for p in bid]
        positions.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
print(f"After DOWN: {sorted(positions)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
